content.text: Document loaders bridge external data sources and LangChain's

attrs.title: Multiple sources feed into loaders which output Document objects

content.text: The most common loaders read documents from the local filesystem.
recursively loads a folder using a per-file-type sub-loader.

# PDF
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader

loader = PyPDFLoader("report.pdf")
docs = loader.load()
print(docs[0].page_content[:200])       # first page text
print(docs[0].metadata)                 # {'source': 'report.pdf', 'page': 0}

# Split pages during load (avoids a separate splitter step for short docs)
docs_with_split = loader.load_and_split()

# DOCX / DOC
from langchain_community.document_loaders import Docx2txtLoader
docs = Docx2txtLoader("spec.docx").load()

# Plain text
from langchain_community.document_loaders import TextLoader
docs = TextLoader("notes.txt", encoding="utf-8").load()

# CSV — each row becomes one Document
from langchain_community.document_loaders.csv_loader import CSVLoader
docs = CSVLoader("products.csv", source_column="product_id").load()
# metadata includes the row's product_id as the source

# Directory — load all PDFs in a folder recursively
from langchain_community.document_loaders import DirectoryLoader
loader = DirectoryLoader(
    "docs/",
    glob="**/*.pdf",
    loader_cls=PyPDFLoader,
    show_progress=True,
    use_multithreading=True,
)
all_docs = loader.load()
print(f"Loaded {len(all_docs)} pages from {len(set(d.metadata['source'] for d in all_docs))} files")
Web loaders fetch remote content and strip HTML, returning clean text.
crawls a site up to a depth limit.

# Single URL
from langchain_community.document_loaders import WebBaseLoader
import bs4

loader = WebBaseLoader(
    web_paths=["https://docs.langchain.com/docs/"],
    bs_kwargs={"parse_only": bs4.SoupStrainer(class_="content")},  # target specific element
)
docs = loader.load()

# Multiple URLs at once
multi_loader = WebBaseLoader([
    "https://example.com/page1",
    "https://example.com/page2",
])
docs = multi_loader.load()

# Recursive site crawler
from langchain_community.document_loaders import RecursiveUrlLoader
from langchain_community.document_loaders.recursive_url_loader import default_extractor

loader = RecursiveUrlLoader(
    url="https://docs.python.org/3/library/",
    max_depth=2,
    extractor=lambda x: default_extractor(x),   # strips tags, returns text
)
docs = list(loader.lazy_load())
print(f"Crawled {len(docs)} pages")
yields documents one at a time rather than loading them all into memory. This is essential when indexing thousands of files.

from langchain_community.document_loaders import DirectoryLoader, PyPDFLoader
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=500, chunk_overlap=50)
loader   = DirectoryLoader("large_corpus/", glob="**/*.pdf", loader_cls=PyPDFLoader)

# Process one document at a time — never hold the full corpus in memory
texts = []
for doc in loader.lazy_load():
    chunks = splitter.split_documents([doc])
    texts.extend(chunks)
    if len(texts) >= 100:
        # Batch index every 100 chunks
        vectorstore.add_documents(texts)
        texts.clear()

if texts:
    vectorstore.add_documents(texts)

# Async lazy loading
async def index_async():
    async for doc in loader.alazy_load():
        chunks = splitter.split_documents([doc])
        await vectorstore.aadd_documents(chunks)
LangChain ships loaders for popular SaaS tools. They authenticate via environment variables and return each item (page, message, issue) as a separate

import os

# Notion — export a database as documents
from langchain_community.document_loaders import NotionDirectoryLoader
# First export your Notion workspace to a folder, then:
docs = NotionDirectoryLoader("notion_export/").load()

# GitHub — load repository files
from langchain_community.document_loaders.github import GithubFileLoader
loader = GithubFileLoader(
    repo="langchain-ai/langchain",
    access_token=os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"],
    github_api_url="https://api.github.com",
    file_filter=lambda f: f.endswith(".md"),   # only markdown files
)
docs = loader.load()

# YouTube transcript
from langchain_community.document_loaders import YoutubeLoader
loader = YoutubeLoader.from_youtube_url(
    "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ",
    add_video_info=True,
    language=["en", "en-US"],
)
docs = loader.load()
print(docs[0].metadata)   # {'title': '...', 'view_count': ..., 'publish_date': ...}

# Slack — export conversation history
from langchain_community.document_loaders import SlackDirectoryLoader
docs = SlackDirectoryLoader("slack_export/", workspace_url="https://myworkspace.slack.com").load()
is automatically derived from it). Add

from langchain_core.document_loaders import BaseLoader
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from typing import Iterator, AsyncIterator
import sqlite3

class SQLiteTableLoader(BaseLoader):
    """Load rows from a SQLite table, one Document per row."""

    def __init__(self, db_path: str, table: str, content_column: str):
        self.db_path       = db_path
        self.table         = table
        self.content_col   = content_column

    def lazy_load(self) -> Iterator[Document]:
        conn   = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)
        cursor = conn.execute(f"SELECT rowid, * FROM {self.table}")
        cols   = [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]

        for row in cursor:
            row_dict = dict(zip(cols, row))
            content  = str(row_dict.pop(self.content_col, ""))
            yield Document(
                page_content=content,
                metadata={"source": f"{self.db_path}:{self.table}", **row_dict},
            )
        conn.close()

# Usage
loader = SQLiteTableLoader("products.db", "reviews", "review_text")
docs   = loader.load()
print(f"Loaded {len(docs)} reviews")

# Or stream them into a retriever
for doc in loader.lazy_load():
    process(doc)
Raw documents are typically too large to fit in a single LLM call.
is the standard splitter — it tries paragraph breaks first, then newlines, then spaces, so chunks stay semantically coherent.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, Language

# Standard text splitting
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=500,
    chunk_overlap=50,
    length_function=len,           # character count; use tiktoken for token count
    add_start_index=True,          # metadata: where in the source doc each chunk starts
)

raw_docs = PyPDFLoader("report.pdf").load()
chunks   = splitter.split_documents(raw_docs)
print(f"{len(raw_docs)} pages → {len(chunks)} chunks")

# Code-aware splitting — respects language structure
code_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_language(
    language=Language.PYTHON,
    chunk_size=800,
    chunk_overlap=80,
)

with open("app.py") as f:
    code = f.read()

code_chunks = code_splitter.create_documents([code])

# Token-based splitting (important when chunk_size maps to LLM context budget)
from langchain_text_splitters import TokenTextSplitter
token_splitter = TokenTextSplitter(chunk_size=256, chunk_overlap=20)
token_chunks   = token_splitter.split_documents(raw_docs)

from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader, DirectoryLoader
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_anthropic import AnthropicEmbeddings
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

# 1. Load
loader = DirectoryLoader("docs/", glob="**/*.pdf", loader_cls=PyPDFLoader)
raw_docs = loader.load()

# 2. Split
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=500, chunk_overlap=50)
chunks = splitter.split_documents(raw_docs)

# 3. Index
embeddings  = AnthropicEmbeddings(model="voyage-3")
vectorstore = FAISS.from_documents(chunks, embeddings)
retriever   = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 4})

# 4. RAG chain
def format_docs(docs): return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Answer using only the context.\n\nContext:\n{context}"),
    ("human", "{question}"),
])

rag_chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(context=retriever | format_docs)
    | prompt
    | ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
    | StrOutputParser()
)

answer = rag_chain.invoke({"question": "What are the key findings?"})
print(answer)
Metadata enables filtered retrieval — for example returning only documents from a specific source, year, or author. You can add or override metadata after loading using a simple

import os
from pathlib import Path
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader, DirectoryLoader

# Add custom metadata during load
loader = DirectoryLoader("reports/", glob="**/*.pdf", loader_cls=PyPDFLoader)
docs   = loader.load()

# Tag each document with department derived from its folder path
for doc in docs:
    path = Path(doc.metadata["source"])
    doc.metadata["department"] = path.parent.name   # e.g. "finance" or "legal"
    doc.metadata["year"]       = int(path.stem[:4]) if path.stem[:4].isdigit() else None
    doc.metadata["doc_type"]   = "report"

# Now retrievers can filter: {"department": "finance", "year": 2024}
finance_docs = [d for d in docs if d.metadata.get("department") == "finance"]

# Tag with file modification time for freshness-aware retrieval
for doc in docs:
    src = doc.metadata["source"]
    doc.metadata["last_modified"] = os.path.getmtime(src)
Test loaders without network calls by mocking the file system or using fixture files. Verify that

import pytest
import tempfile, os
from langchain_community.document_loaders import TextLoader, CSVLoader

@pytest.fixture
def sample_txt(tmp_path):
    p = tmp_path / "sample.txt"
    p.write_text("Hello world\nThis is a test document.")
    return str(p)

def test_text_loader_content(sample_txt):
    docs = TextLoader(sample_txt).load()
    assert len(docs) == 1
    assert "Hello world" in docs[0].page_content
    assert docs[0].metadata["source"] == sample_txt

def test_text_loader_encoding(tmp_path):
    p = tmp_path / "unicode.txt"
    p.write_text("Héllo wörld — résumé", encoding="utf-8")
    docs = TextLoader(str(p), encoding="utf-8").load()
    assert "résumé" in docs[0].page_content

@pytest.fixture
def sample_csv(tmp_path):
    p = tmp_path / "data.csv"
    p.write_text("id,review\n1,Great product\n2,Needs improvement")
    return str(p)

def test_csv_loader_rows(sample_csv):
    docs = CSVLoader(sample_csv, source_column="id").load()
    assert len(docs) == 2
    assert "Great product" in docs[0].page_content
    assert docs[0].metadata["source"] == "1"

# Test lazy_load yields documents incrementally
def test_lazy_load_yields_one_at_a_time(sample_txt):
    loader = TextLoader(sample_txt)
    gen = loader.lazy_load()
    first = next(gen)
    assert first.page_content != ""
    # Generator should be exhausted after one item for a single-file loader
    with pytest.raises(StopIteration):
        next(gen)
(returns generator) — use lazy loading for large corpora to avoid memory pressure.
when chunks must fit a token budget.
